preamble: The shift from the close front rounded vowel [y] to the open-mid back unrounded vowel [ʌ] typically occurs through a multi-stage process of unrounding and lowering. This historical trajectory is a hallmark of the evolution of the English vowel system from Old English to the modern era.

examples.note: Historical chain shift: The Old English high front rounded vowel [y] unrounded and retracted in some dialects to [ʊ], which then participated in the Foot-Strut Split of the 17th century to become the modern [ʌ].
Related developments where original [y] ended up in the [ʌ] or [ɪ] space.

Rounding and backness harmony: In suffix vowels, the front rounded [y] alternates with the mid-back unrounded [ʌ] (realization of /ɯ/) when the root vowel class changes from front-rounded to back-unrounded.
